name: Fork-Join Parallelism

x.coordination: A task recursively forks subtasks, which run in parallel, then joins (waits) for their results before combining — a structured, nested parallel decomposition.
hazards: Blocking inside tasks can deadlock the pool

def: A structured model where a problem is recursively split into subtasks that run in parallel and are then joined, ideal for divide-and-conquer algorithms.
problem: Parallelise recursive divide-and-conquer work while keeping cores busy and the structure easy to reason about.
mechanism: fork submits subtasks to a pool (usually with work-stealing deques for load balancing); join waits for them and combines results. A cutoff switches to sequential execution for small subproblems to amortise overhead.
failures: Tiny tasks where overhead beats useful work
Blocking I/O inside compute tasks stalling the pool
myths: "More forking = more speed" — past a point, overhead and contention dominate
